nodes.name: Countries of the World
nodes.description: Country sets by region and category

config.prompt: Name all landlocked countries of the world (no ocean coastline)

items.explanation:
Central Asia; capital Kabul
Bordered by Pakistan, Iran, Turkmenistan, Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, and China
One of the most landlocked countries in Asia — nearest ocean access is ~500 km away
Historically called the 'graveyard of empires' for resisting conquest by British, Soviet, and U.S. forces

Micro-state in the eastern Pyrenees; capital Andorra la Vella
Bordered only by France and Spain; area of just 468 km²
Andorra la Vella is the highest-elevation capital city in Europe at ~1,023 m
No army, no airport; relies heavily on tax-free shopping and ski tourism

South Caucasus; capital Yerevan
Bordered by Turkey, Georgia, Azerbaijan, and Iran; formerly had Black Sea access via Georgia
First country in the world to adopt Christianity as its state religion (301 CE)
Mount Ararat, the national symbol, is visible from Yerevan but lies inside Turkey

Central Europe; capital Vienna
Bordered by Germany, Czech Republic, Slovakia, Hungary, Slovenia, Italy, Switzerland, and Liechtenstein
Once the center of the Habsburg Empire, which dominated Central Europe for 600 years
Vienna was the most populous German-speaking city in the world until the 20th century

South Caucasus; capital Baku
Borders the Caspian Sea, but the Caspian is a sea/lake — no ocean coastline
Bordered by Russia, Georgia, Armenia, Iran, and the Caspian Sea
Baku sits below sea level (−28 m) and is the lowest-elevation national capital in the world
Major oil producer; oil rigs visible offshore in the Caspian

Eastern Europe; capital Minsk
Bordered by Russia, Ukraine, Poland, Lithuania, and Latvia
One of the last European dictatorships; Alexander Lukashenko has ruled since 1994
Heavily dependent on Russia for energy and trade

South Asia, eastern Himalayas; capital Thimphu
Bordered by China and India; strictly limited tourism to protect culture and environment
Measures 'Gross National Happiness' as an official government policy metric
One of the few countries to have never been colonized

South America; constitutional capital Sucre, seat of government La Paz
Lost its Pacific coastline to Chile in the War of the Pacific (1879–1884)
Despite being landlocked, Bolivia maintains a navy — the 'Sea Day' holiday (March 23) commemorates the loss
Lake Titicaca (shared with Peru) is the world's highest navigable lake at 3,812 m above sea level

Southern Africa; capital Gaborone
One of the world's largest diamond producers — Debswana (De Beers + Botswana govt) is the primary mine
At independence (1966) was one of Africa's poorest nations; now among the wealthiest per capita on the continent
The Okavango Delta, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, is the world's largest inland delta

West Africa; capital Ouagadougou
Formerly called Upper Volta; renamed Burkina Faso ('Land of Upright People') in 1984 by revolutionary president Thomas Sankara
Sankara was assassinated in 1987 in a coup led by Blaise Compaoré
One of the poorest countries in the world; heavily affected by Sahel droughts and jihadist insurgency since 2015

Central/East Africa; capital Gitega (formerly Bujumbura)
One of the world's most densely populated countries and among the poorest
Ethnic tensions between Hutu and Tutsi populations led to civil war (1993–2005)
Capital moved from Bujumbura to Gitega in 2019

Central Africa; capital Bangui
Consistently ranked among the lowest on the UN Human Development Index
Rich in diamonds, gold, uranium, and timber despite extreme poverty
Has endured multiple coups and prolonged civil war since 2012

Central Africa; capital N'Djamena
Lake Chad, once one of Africa's largest lakes, has shrunk by ~90% since the 1960s due to climate change and irrigation
Bordered by Libya, Sudan, Central African Republic, Cameroon, Nigeria, and Niger
One of the hottest countries on Earth — parts of the Sahara reach 50 °C

Central Europe; capital Prague
Formed in 1993 when Czechoslovakia peacefully split into Czech Republic and Slovakia ('Velvet Divorce')
Prague's historic center is a UNESCO World Heritage Site; the city was largely undamaged in WWII
Highest beer consumption per capita in the world

Southern Africa; capital Mbabane (administrative), Lobamba (royal/legislative)
Completely enclosed within South Africa, with a small border with Mozambique
Renamed from Swaziland to Eswatini ('Land of the Swazis') in 2018 by King Mswati III
One of the world's last absolute monarchies

East Africa; capital Addis Ababa
World's most populous landlocked country — over 120 million people
Lost sea access when Eritrea gained independence in 1993 after a 30-year war
Addis Ababa hosts the African Union headquarters; often called the 'diplomatic capital of Africa'
One of two African countries never colonized (alongside Liberia)

Central Europe; capital Budapest
Bordered by Austria, Slovakia, Ukraine, Romania, Serbia, Croatia, and Slovenia
The Danube splits Budapest — Buda on the west bank, Pest on the east — unified as one city in 1873
Part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire until its dissolution after WWI (1918)

Central Asia; capital Astana (formerly Nur-Sultan, formerly Astana)
World's largest landlocked country by area at ~2.7 million km² — larger than Western Europe
Borders Russia, China, Kyrgyzstan, Uzbekistan, Turkmenistan, and the Caspian Sea
Home to the Baikonur Cosmodrome, the world's first and largest operational space launch facility
Ninth-largest country in the world overall

Central Asia; capital Bishkek
Bordered by Kazakhstan, China, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan
Farthest landlocked country from any ocean — over 2,500 km from the nearest sea
Lake Issyk-Kul is the second-largest alpine lake in the world and never freezes despite high altitude

Southeast Asia; capital Vientiane
Only landlocked country in Southeast Asia
Bordered by China, Vietnam, Cambodia, Thailand, and Myanmar
Most heavily bombed country per capita in history — the U.S. dropped more ordnance on Laos during the Vietnam War era than on Germany in WWII
Vientiane is the least-visited capital city in Southeast Asia

Southern Africa; capital Maseru
Completely surrounded by South Africa — one of only three countries in the world entirely enclosed within another country (with Vatican City and San Marino)
Entire country lies above 1,000 m elevation; nicknamed 'Kingdom in the Sky'
King Letsie III is the reigning monarch

Western Europe; capital Vaduz
One of only two doubly landlocked countries in the world (the other is Uzbekistan) — surrounded entirely by landlocked Austria and Switzerland
Second-smallest German-speaking country after Andorra; area of just 160 km²
Despite tiny size, has one of the world's highest GDP per capita — major financial center
Has no airport; nearest airports are in Zürich and Vienna

Western Europe; capital Luxembourg City
Bordered by Belgium, France, and Germany
One of the world's wealthiest countries per capita — major European financial and EU administrative center
Luxembourg City's old fortifications are a UNESCO World Heritage Site
EU founding member; home to the European Court of Justice and Court of Auditors

East Africa; capital Lilongwe
Formerly called Nyasaland under British colonial rule
Lake Malawi (Lake Nyasa) covers about a third of the country's area and contains more fish species than any other lake on Earth
One of the most densely populated countries in sub-Saharan Africa

West Africa; capital Bamako
Home to Timbuktu (Tombouctou), a medieval center of Islamic scholarship and gold trade
The ancient Mali Empire (13th–17th century) was one of the wealthiest in the world under Mansa Musa
Experienced multiple coups; insurgency in the north linked to al-Qaeda and ISIS affiliates since 2012

Eastern Europe; capital Chișinău
Bordered by Romania and Ukraine; was part of the USSR until 1991
Separatist region of Transnistria broke away in 1992 and remains de facto independent, backed by Russia
One of the least visited and poorest countries in Europe

East Asia; capital Ulaanbaytar
World's second-largest landlocked country by area at ~1.56 million km²
Bordered only by Russia and China
Home of the Mongol Empire (13th–14th century), the largest contiguous land empire in history under Genghis Khan
Ulaanbaatar is the world's coldest national capital, with average annual temperatures below freezing

South Asia, central Himalayas; capital Kathmandu
Home to 8 of the world's 10 highest mountains, including Mount Everest (8,849 m)
Bordered by China and India only
Became a republic in 2008 after 240 years of monarchy
Sir Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay made the first confirmed Everest summit on May 29, 1953

West Africa; capital Niamey
Largest country in West Africa by area; ~80% is Sahara desert
Bordered by Algeria, Libya, Chad, Nigeria, Benin, Burkina Faso, and Mali
One of the lowest-ranked countries on the Human Development Index
Military coup in July 2023 ousted President Mohamed Bazoum

Southeast Europe (Balkans); capital Skopje
Renamed from 'Macedonia' to 'North Macedonia' in 2019 under the Prespa Agreement with Greece, which had objected to the name
Bordered by Serbia, Kosovo, Bulgaria, Greece, and Albania
Joined NATO in 2020 shortly after the name change

South America; capital Asunción
One of only two landlocked countries in South America (along with Bolivia)
Bordered by Argentina, Bolivia, and Brazil
The Itaipu Dam on the Paraná River (shared with Brazil) was the world's largest hydroelectric plant by output for most of 1984–2016
Guaraní is co-official with Spanish — one of few countries where an indigenous language is widely spoken by the general population

Central/East Africa; capital Kigali
In 1994, the Rwandan genocide killed an estimated 500,000–800,000 Tutsi and moderate Hutu in roughly 100 days
Now one of Africa's fastest-growing economies and considered one of its cleanest, safest cities
Highest proportion of women in parliament of any country in the world

Southern Europe; capital San Marino (City of San Marino)
Completely surrounded by Italy — one of three countries entirely enclosed within another country
Claims to be the world's oldest republic, founded in 301 CE
Smallest population of any member of the Council of Europe; area of just 61 km²

Southeast Europe (Balkans); capital Belgrade
Became landlocked in 2006 when Montenegro declared independence (Serbia lost its Adriatic coastline)
Bordered by Hungary, Romania, Bulgaria, North Macedonia, Kosovo, Bosnia-Herzegovina, Croatia, and Montenegro
Belgrade sits at the confluence of the Sava and Danube rivers and is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in Europe

Central Europe; capital Bratislava
Formed in 1993 when Czechoslovakia split into Slovakia and Czech Republic ('Velvet Divorce')
Bratislava is the only national capital that borders two different countries (Austria and Hungary)
Bordered by Czech Republic, Poland, Ukraine, Hungary, and Austria

East/Central Africa; capital Juba
World's newest country — gained independence from Sudan on July 9, 2011
Almost immediately descended into civil war (2013–2020) along ethnic lines
Despite significant oil reserves, one of the poorest and least developed countries in the world
Bordered by Sudan, Ethiopia, Kenya, Uganda, DRC, and Central African Republic

Central/Western Europe; capital Bern (federal city)
Bordered by Germany, Austria, Liechtenstein, Italy, and France
Neutrality maintained since 1815 — not a member of the EU; joined the UN only in 2002
Home to the International Red Cross (founded Geneva, 1863) and numerous international organizations
Geneva and Zurich are consistently ranked among the world's top cities for quality of life

Central Asia; capital Dushanbe
Bordered by Afghanistan, China, Kyrgyzstan, and Uzbekistan
Highest mountain passes in Central Asia; over 90% of the country is mountainous
Suffered a brutal civil war from 1992 to 1997 after independence from the USSR
Poor in natural resources compared to other Central Asian states

Central Asia; capital Ashgabat
Borders the Caspian Sea but has no ocean access — the Caspian is classified as a lake/sea
Home to the 'Door to Hell' (Darvaza gas crater), a burning natural gas crater lit since 1971
Among the world's most isolated and authoritarian states; has among the world's largest natural gas reserves
Prizes white marble buildings — Ashgabat holds the record for most white marble-clad buildings in a city

East Africa; capital Kampala
Bordered by Sudan, Kenya, Tanzania, Rwanda, and DRC; contains much of Lake Victoria, the world's largest tropical lake
Source of the White Nile is at Jinja, where the river flows out of Lake Victoria
Ruled brutally by Idi Amin from 1971–1979; an estimated 100,000–500,000 people were killed

Central Asia; capital Tashkent
One of only two doubly landlocked countries in the world (the other is Liechtenstein) — surrounded entirely by other landlocked countries: Afghanistan, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, and Turkmenistan
Samarkand and Bukhara were major centers of the Silk Road
Home to the Aral Sea, once the world's fourth-largest lake — shrunken by ~90% due to Soviet-era irrigation diversions
Tashkent is Central Asia's largest city

Southern Europe; capital is Vatican City itself
Completely surrounded by Rome, Italy — world's smallest sovereign state at 0.44 km²
Seat of the Roman Catholic Church; home of the Pope
St. Peter's Basilica and the Sistine Chapel (ceiling painted by Michelangelo, 1508–1512) are within its walls
Established as an independent state by the Lateran Treaty with Italy in 1929

Southern Africa; capital Lusaka
Formerly Northern Rhodesia; gained independence from Britain in 1964
Victoria Falls ('Mosi-oa-Tunya'), one of the world's largest waterfalls, sits on the Zambia–Zimbabwe border
One of the world's top copper producers

Southern Africa; capital Harare
Formerly Rhodesia; achieved majority rule and renamed Zimbabwe in 1980 under Robert Mugabe
Once called the 'breadbasket of Africa'; hyperinflation under Mugabe (peak: 89.7 sextillion percent in 2008) destroyed the economy
Victoria Falls sits on its border with Zambia
Mugabe ruled until a military coup forced his resignation in 2017
